html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Two

The Book of Esther

ESTHER

Covenant History — Orit Ge'ez Primary Authority

Chapters 1 through 10

Your People Shall Be My People — If I Perish I Perish — Who Knows Whether You Have Come for Such a Time as This — The Month Turned From Sorrow Into Gladness

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Orit Ge'ez — Ethiopian Complete Canon — Primary Authority

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

ESTHER — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 10 — THE COVENANT COMMUNITY IN THE PERSIAN DIASPORA

ESTHER — CHAPTER 1 King Akhashverosh — The Feast of Shushan — Queen Vashti Refuses — The King's Anger — Let Her Royal Position Be Given to Another

1 Now in the days of Akhashverosh, the Akhashverosh who reigned from Hodu to Kush over 127 provinces,

2 In those days when King Akhashverosh sat on his royal throne in Shushan the citadel,

3 In the third year of his reign he gave a feast for all his officials and servants. The army of Persia and Medes and the nobles and governors of the provinces were before him,

4 While he showed the riches of his royal glory and the splendor and pomp of his greatness for many days, 180 days.

5 And when these days were completed, the king gave for all the people present in Shushan the citadel, both great and small, a feast lasting for seven days in the court of the garden of the king's palace.

6 There were white cotton curtains and violet hangings fastened with cords of fine linen and purple to silver rods and marble pillars, and also couches of gold and silver on a mosaic pavement of porphyry, marble, mother-of-pearl, and precious stones.

7 Drinks were served in golden vessels, vessels of different kinds, and the royal wine was lavished according to the bounty of the king.

8 And drinking was according to this edict: there is no compulsion. For the king had given orders to all the staff of his palace to do as each man desired.

9 Queen Vashti also gave a feast for the women in the palace that belonged to King Akhashverosh.

10 On the seventh day, when the heart of the king was merry with wine, he commanded Mehuman, Biztha, Harvona, Bigtha and Avagtha, Zetar and Karkas, the seven eunuchs who served in the presence of King Akhashverosh,

11 To bring Queen Vashti before the king with her royal crown, in order to show the peoples and the princes her beauty, for she was lovely to look at.

12 But Queen Vashti refused to come at the king's command delivered by the eunuchs. At this the king became enraged, and his anger burned within him.

13 Then the king said to the wise men who knew the times — for this was the king's procedure toward all who were versed in law and judgment,

14 The men next to him being Karshena, Shetar, Admata, Tarshish, Meres, Marsena, and Memukhan, the seven princes of Persia and Medes, who saw the king's face, and sat first in the kingdom —

15 What is to be done to Queen Vashti, according to the law, because she has not performed the command of King Akhashverosh delivered by the eunuchs?

16 Then Memukhan said in the presence of the king and the officials, Not only against the king has Queen Vashti done wrong, but also against all the officials and all the peoples who are in all the provinces of King Akhashverosh.

17 For the queen's behavior will be made known to all women, causing them to look at their husbands with contempt, since they will say, King Akhashverosh commanded Queen Vashti to be brought before him, and she did not come.

18 This very day the noble women of Persia and Medes who have heard of the queen's behavior will say the same to all the king's officials, and there will be contempt and wrath in plenty.

19 If it please the king, let a royal order go out from him, and let it be written among the laws of the Persians and the Medes so that it may not be repealed, that Vashti is never again to come before King Akhashverosh. And let the king give her royal position to another who is better than she.

20 So when the decree made by the king is proclaimed throughout all his kingdom, for it is vast, all women will give honor to their husbands, high and low alike.

21 This advice pleased the king and the princes, and the king did as Memukhan proposed.

22 He sent letters to all the royal provinces, to every province in its own script and to every people in its own language, that every man be master in his own household and speak according to the language of his people.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No substantial Hebrew or Aramaic Esther text was found among the Dead Sea Scrolls. The absence is noted: every other book of the Hebrew canon is represented at Qumran except Esther. The Orit Ge'ez stands as the primary ancient textual witness for this volume alongside the Masoretic. The Ge'ez tradition preserves Esther as a complete and authoritative covenant text with additional material reflecting early traditions absent from the shorter Masoretic recension.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Esther Chapter 1 in full as the primary authority for this volume. The Ge'ez tradition presents the book within the Afro-Asiatic covenant corridor context: the Persian empire from Hodu to Kush — verse 1 — encompasses the northeast African and South Asian extent of Persian power. The covenant community of Yisra'EL exists within this vast imperial context under covenant conditions of diaspora.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 1 as the foundation of the Esther narrative. Verse 12 — Queen Vashti refused to come at the king's command. The narrative pivot: Vashti's refusal sets in motion the covenant chain of events that will bring the covenant community's deliverer to the covenant position of power. The covenant removal of Vashti is the covenant preparation for the covenant elevation of Hadassah — Esther.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Chapter 1 is notable for what is absent: the name of YHWH does not appear in the entire book of Esther in the Masoretic text. The Orit Ge'ez tradition supplements with the prayers of Mordekhai and Esther that make the covenant divine presence explicit. The covenant hiddenness of YHWH in the Masoretic Esther is the covenant literary mirror of the covenant hiddenness of the covenant community in the diaspora: YHWH operating through covenant events without covenant visible covenant announcement.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 2 Let Beautiful Young Virgins Be Sought — Hadassah Who Is Esther — She Had Neither Father Nor Mother — Mordekhai Walked Before the Court — Esther Found Favor — The Conspiracy of Two Eunuchs

1 After these things, when the anger of King Akhashverosh had abated, he remembered Vashti and what she had done and what had been decreed against her.

2 Then the king's young men who attended him said, Let beautiful young virgins be sought out for the king.

3 And let the king appoint officers in all the provinces of his kingdom to gather all the beautiful young virgins to Shushan the citadel, under custody of Hegai, the king's eunuch, who is in charge of the women. Let their cosmetics be given them.

4 And let the young woman who pleases the king be queen instead of Vashti. This pleased the king, and he did so.

5 Now there was a Yehudi man in Shushan the citadel whose name was Mordekhai, the son of Ya'ir, son of Shim'i, son of Kish, a Binyaminite,

6 Who had been carried away from Yerushalayim among the captives carried away with Yekonyahu king of Yehudah, whom Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon had carried away.

7 He was bringing up Hadassah, that is Esther, the daughter of his uncle, for she had neither father nor mother. The young woman had a beautiful figure and was lovely to look at, and when her father and her mother died, Mordekhai took her as his own daughter.

8 So when the king's order and his edict were proclaimed, and when many young women were gathered in Shushan the citadel in custody of Hegai, Esther also was taken into the king's palace and put in custody of Hegai, who had charge of the women.

9 And the young woman pleased him and won his favor. And he quickly provided her with her cosmetics and her portion of food, and with seven chosen young women from the king's palace, and advanced her and her young women to the best place in the harem.

10 Esther had not made known her people or kindred, for Mordekhai had commanded her not to make it known.

11 And every day Mordekhai walked in front of the court of the harem to learn how Esther was and what was happening to her.

12 Now when the turn came for each young woman to go in to King Akhashverosh, after being twelve months under the regulations for the women — since this was the regular period of their beautifying, six months with oil of myrrh and six months with spices and ointments for women —

13 When the young woman went in to the king in this way, she was given whatever she desired to take with her from the harem to the king's palace.

14 In the evening she would go in, and in the morning she would return to the second harem in custody of Sha'ashgaz, the king's eunuch, who was in charge of the concubines. She would not go in to the king again, unless the king delighted in her and she was summoned by name.

15 When the turn came for Esther the daughter of Avihayil the uncle of Mordekhai, who had taken her as his own daughter, to go in to the king, she asked for nothing except what Hegai the king's eunuch, who had charge of the women, advised. Now Esther was winning favor in the eyes of all who saw her.

16 And Esther was taken to King Akhashverosh into his royal palace in the tenth month, which is the month of Tevet, in the seventh year of his reign.

17 The king loved Esther more than all the women, and she won grace and favor in his sight more than all the virgins, so that he set the royal crown on her head and made her queen instead of Vashti.

18 Then the king gave a great feast for all his officials and servants. It was Esther's feast. He also granted a remission of taxes to the provinces and gave gifts with royal generosity.

19 Now when the virgins were gathered together the second time, Mordekhai was sitting at the king's gate.

20 Esther had not made known her kindred or her people, as Mordekhai had commanded her, for Esther obeyed Mordekhai just as when she was brought up by him.

21 In those days, as Mordekhai was sitting at the king's gate, Bigtan and Teresh, two of the king's eunuchs, who guarded the threshold, became angry and sought to lay hands on King Akhashverosh.

22 And this came to the knowledge of Mordekhai, and he told it to Queen Esther, and Esther told the king in the name of Mordekhai.

23 When the affair was investigated and found to be so, the men were both hanged on the gallows. And it was recorded in the book of the chronicles in the presence of the king.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority for this volume. Chapter 2 introduces the covenant community's condition within the Persian diaspora: concealed covenant identity — verse 10, 20 — covenant daily vigilance — Mordekhai walking before the court, verse 11 — and covenant providential positioning through what appears to be purely political process. The covenant community survives through covenant wisdom and covenant concealment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full with additional material in the Ge'ez tradition regarding Esther's covenant preparation and covenant prayer before entering the covenant royal process. Verse 7 — he was bringing up Hadassah, that is Esther — the covenant double name: Hadassah is the covenant Hebrew name — myrtle — while Esther is the covenant Persian name. The covenant community member in diaspora operates with a covenant double identity.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 10 — Esther had not made known her people or kindred, for Mordekhai had commanded her not to make it known. The covenant concealment of covenant identity is the covenant strategic decision that will be dramatically reversed in Chapter 4. The covenant timing of covenant identity disclosure is the covenant narrative axis of the entire book.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 22-23 — Mordekhai reported the assassination plot, it was recorded in the book of the chronicles. The covenant registration of the covenant service that goes unacknowledged at the time — verse 6:1-3 will return to this entry when the king cannot sleep and has the chronicles read aloud. The covenant book of chronicles preserves what covenant memory overlooked. The covenant service unacknowledged in covenant human time is covenant preserved in the covenant historical record for its covenant appointed moment.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 3 Haman the Agagite Promoted — Mordekhai Would Not Bow — Haman Sought to Destroy All the Yehudim — The Letters Sent to All Provinces — Destroy Kill and Annihilate

1 After these things King Akhashverosh promoted Haman the Agagite, the son of Hammedata, and advanced him and set his throne above all the officials who were with him.

2 And all the king's servants who were at the king's gate bowed down and paid homage to Haman, for the king had so commanded concerning him. But Mordekhai did not bow down or pay homage.

3 Then the king's servants who were at the king's gate said to Mordekhai, Why do you transgress the king's command?

4 And when they spoke to him day after day and he would not listen to them, they told Haman, in order to see whether Mordekhai's words would stand, for he had told them that he was a Yehudi.

5 And when Haman saw that Mordekhai did not bow down or pay homage to him, Haman was filled with fury.

6 But he disdained to lay hands on Mordekhai alone. So, as they had made known to him the people of Mordekhai, Haman sought to destroy all the Yehudim, the people of Mordekhai, throughout the whole kingdom of Akhashverosh.

7 In the first month, which is the month of Nisan, in the twelfth year of King Akhashverosh, they cast Pur — that is, they cast lots — before Haman day after day and month after month till the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar.

8 Then Haman said to King Akhashverosh, There is a certain people scattered abroad and dispersed among the peoples in all the provinces of your kingdom. Their laws are different from those of every other people, and they do not keep the king's laws, so that it is not to the king's profit to tolerate them.

9 If it please the king, let it be decreed that they be destroyed, and I will pay 10,000 talents of silver into the hands of those who have charge of the king's business, that they may put it into the king's treasuries.

10 So the king took his signet ring from his hand and gave it to Haman the Agagite, the son of Hammedata, the enemy of the Yehudim.

11 And the king said to Haman, The money is given to you, the people also, to do with them as it seems good to you.

12 Then the king's scribes were summoned on the thirteenth day of the first month, and an edict, according to all that Haman commanded, was written to the king's satraps and to the governors over all the provinces and to the officials of all the peoples, to every province in its own script and every people in its own language. It was written in the name of King Akhashverosh and sealed with the king's signet ring.

13 Letters were sent by couriers to all the king's provinces with instruction to destroy, to kill, and to annihilate all Yehudim, young and old, women and children, in one day, the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar, and to plunder their goods.

14 A copy of the document was to be issued as a decree in every province by proclamation to all the peoples, to be ready for that day.

15 The couriers went out hurriedly by order of the king, and the decree was issued in Shushan the citadel. And the king and Haman sat down to drink, but the city of Shushan was thrown into confusion.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. Chapter 3 introduces the covenant enemy: Haman the Agagite — a descendant of Agag king of Amalek — Shemu'el Aleph 15 — the ancient covenant enemy of Yisra'EL. The covenant conflict is the covenant continuation of the Saul-Agag covenant failure: Sha'ul did not completely destroy the Amalekites as YHWH commanded, and Agag's covenant line now seeks to do what Pharaoh could not: annihilate the entire covenant people in one day.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 8 — there is a certain people scattered abroad and dispersed — their laws are different from those of every other people. The covenant accusation that has pursued the covenant diaspora community throughout history: covenant distinctiveness framed as covenant disloyalty. Haman's covenant indictment of the covenant community is the covenant classic accusation of every covenant anti-covenant power: they are different, therefore they are dangerous.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 2 — Mordekhai did not bow down or pay homage. The covenant refusal that triggers the covenant crisis: the entire covenant community's covenant survival hangs on Mordekhai's covenant refusal to give Haman the honor he demands. The covenant narrative does not explain Mordekhai's covenant reason — it presents the covenant refusal as the covenant given from which everything else follows.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — they cast Pur — that is, they cast lots. The covenant enemy uses covenant pagan divination — lot-casting — to determine the optimal date for the covenant annihilation. The covenant irony: the covenant lottery system that Haman uses to choose the date for covenant destruction is the same covenant mechanism that gives the covenant community the maximum time to respond — the lot falls on the twelfth month, giving eleven months of covenant preparation time. The covenant pagan divination system works against its covenant user.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 4 Mordekhai Tore His Clothes — The Queen Was Deeply Distressed — If I Perish I Perish — Who Knows Whether You Have Not Come to the Kingdom for Such a Time as This

1 When Mordekhai learned all that had been done, Mordekhai tore his clothes and put on sackcloth and ashes, and went out into the midst of the city, and he cried out with a loud and bitter cry.

2 He went up to the entrance of the king's gate, for no one was allowed to enter the king's gate clothed in sackcloth.

3 And in every province, wherever the king's command and his decree reached, there was great mourning among the Yehudim, with fasting and weeping and lamenting, and many of them lay in sackcloth and ashes.

4 When Esther's young women and her eunuchs came and told her, the queen was deeply distressed. She sent garments to clothe Mordekhai, so that he might take off his sackcloth, but he would not accept them.

5 Then Esther called for Hatakh, one of the king's eunuchs, who had been appointed to attend her, and ordered him to go to Mordekhai to learn what this was and why it was.

6 Hatakh went out to Mordekhai in the open square of the city in front of the king's gate,

7 And Mordekhai told him all that had happened to him, and the exact sum of money that Haman had promised to pay into the king's treasuries for the destruction of the Yehudim.

8 Mordekhai also gave him a copy of the written decree issued in Shushan for their destruction, that he might show it to Esther and explain it to her and command her to go to the king to beg his favor and plead with him on behalf of her people.

9 And Hatakh went and told Esther what Mordekhai had said.

10 Then Esther spoke to Hatakh and commanded him to go to Mordekhai and say,

11 All the king's servants and the people of the king's provinces know that if any man or woman goes to the king inside the inner court without being called, there is but one law — to be put to death, except the one to whom the king holds out the golden scepter so that he may live. But as for me, I have not been called to come in to the king these thirty days.

12 And they told Mordekhai what Esther had said.

13 Then Mordekhai told them to reply to Esther, Do not think to yourself that in the king's palace you will escape any more than all the other Yehudim.

14 For if you keep silent at this time, relief and deliverance will rise for the Yehudim from another place, but you and your father's house will perish. And who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?

15 Then Esther told them to reply to Mordekhai,

16 Go, gather all the Yehudim to be found in Shushan, and hold a fast on my behalf, and do not eat or drink for three days, night or day. I and my young women will also fast as you do. Then I will go to the king, though it is against the law, and if I perish, I perish.

17 Mordekhai then went away and did everything as Esther had ordered him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. The Ge'ez tradition preserves the prayer of Mordekhai and the prayer of Esther that fill the three-day fast of verse 16 with explicit covenant content. In the Ge'ez tradition, Esther prays to YHWH directly — calling on the covenant God of Avraham, Yitzkhak, and Yaakov — making covenant explicit what the Masoretic leaves covenant implicit. The Orit covenant prayers make the covenant theological dimension of the covenant fast unmistakable.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full with the prayer traditions. Verse 14 — who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this? The covenant question that is the theological center of the entire book: covenant providence operating through covenant human agency at a covenant critical moment. The covenant rhetorical question implies a covenant affirmative answer without covenant stating it.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 16 — if I perish, I perish. The covenant sentence that defines Esther's covenant transformation from covenant passive covenant beneficiary to covenant active covenant agent. The covenant woman who concealed her covenant identity now risks her covenant life for her covenant people. The covenant concealment phase ends — the covenant disclosure and covenant advocacy phase begins.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13-14 — do not think you will escape in the king's palace. If you keep silent at this time, relief and deliverance will rise for the Yehudim from another place, but you and your father's house will perish. Mordekhai's covenant argument has two parts: the covenant community will be delivered — the covenant certainty of covenant rescue — and Esther's covenant choice is whether to be the covenant instrument of that rescue or miss the covenant moment. The covenant rescue is covenant certain; the covenant agent is covenant at stake.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 5 Esther Approached the King — He Held Out the Golden Scepter — What Is Your Request? — Let the King and Haman Come to the Feast — Haman Goes Out Joyful — The Gallows Fifty Cubits High

1 On the third day Esther put on her royal robes and stood in the inner court of the king's palace, in front of the king's quarters, while the king was sitting on his royal throne inside the throne room opposite the entrance to the palace.

2 And when the king saw Queen Esther standing in the court, she won favor in his sight, and he held out to Esther the golden scepter that was in his hand. Then Esther approached and touched the tip of the scepter.

3 And the king said to her, What is it, Queen Esther? What is your request? It shall be given you, even to the half of my kingdom.

4 And Esther said, If it please the king, let the king and Haman come today to a feast that I have prepared for the king.

5 Then the king said, Bring Haman quickly, so that we may do as Esther has asked. So the king and Haman came to the feast that Esther had prepared.

6 And as they were drinking wine after the feast, the king said to Esther, What is your wish? It shall be granted you. And what is your request? Even to the half of my kingdom, it shall be fulfilled.

7 Then Esther answered, My wish and my request is:

8 If I have found favor in the sight of the king, and if it please the king to grant my wish and fulfill my request, let the king and Haman come to the feast that I will prepare for them, and tomorrow I will do as the king has said.

9 And Haman went out that day joyful and glad of heart. But when Haman saw Mordekhai in the king's gate, that he neither rose nor trembled before him, he was filled with wrath against Mordekhai.

10 Nevertheless, Haman restrained himself and went home, and he sent and brought his friends and his wife Zeresh.

11 And Haman recounted to them the splendor of his riches, the number of his sons, all the promotions with which the king had honored him, and how he had advanced him above the officials and the servants of the king.

12 Then Haman said, Even Queen Esther let no one but me come with the king to the feast she prepared. And tomorrow also I am invited by her together with the king.

13 Yet all this is worth nothing to me, so long as I see Mordekhai the Yehudi sitting at the king's gate.

14 Then his wife Zeresh and all his friends said to him, Let a gallows fifty cubits high be made, and in the morning tell the king to have Mordekhai hanged upon it. Then go joyfully with the king to the feast. This idea pleased Haman, and he had the gallows made.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. The covenant delayed revelation strategy of Esther — inviting the king and Haman to not one but two feasts before making her covenant request — is the covenant wisdom of the covenant diaspora community: measured covenant timing, covenant patient covenant execution, creating covenant maximum conditions for the covenant request. The covenant wisdom of Esther in the covenant dangerous environment of the Persian court is the covenant wisdom of the covenant people in covenant exile.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 2 — he held out to Esther the golden scepter. The Ge'ez tradition notes that Esther's covenant survival of the unsummoned approach to the king is read as a covenant sign of YHWH's covenant protection — the explicit covenant prayer of the Ge'ez addition finds its covenant answer in the extended scepter. The covenant risk of Chapter 4:16 produces the covenant miracle of Chapter 5:2.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 13 — all this is worth nothing to me so long as I see Mordekhai the Yehudi sitting at the king's gate. The covenant psychological portrait of Haman: the totality of his covenant power, covenant wealth, and covenant honor cannot overcome the covenant irritant of one covenant Yehudi's refusal to bow. Covenant honor demands covenant universal submission — any covenant exception nullifies the covenant whole.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — let a gallows fifty cubits high be made. The covenant overreach of covenant pride: Haman builds a gallows of exaggerated height to make a public covenant spectacle of Mordekhai's covenant execution. The covenant irony that pervades the entire book: the covenant instrument Haman builds for Mordekhai becomes the covenant instrument of Haman's own destruction. The covenant gallows will be used exactly as covenant planned — with a different covenant occupant.

THE GREAT REVERSAL — CHAPTERS 6 THROUGH 10

ESTHER — CHAPTER 6 The King Could Not Sleep — The Chronicles Were Read — What Honor Has Been Done to Mordekhai? — Nothing Has Been Done — Haman Comes to the Court — Thus Shall It Be Done to the Man the King Delights to Honor

1 On that night the king could not sleep. And he gave orders to bring the book of memorable deeds, the chronicles, and they were read before the king.

2 And it was found written how Mordekhai had told about Bigtan and Teresh, two of the king's eunuchs, who guarded the threshold, and who had sought to lay hands on King Akhashverosh.

3 And the king said, What honor or distinction has been bestowed on Mordekhai for this? The king's young men who attended him said, Nothing has been done for him.

4 And the king said, Who is in the court? Now Haman had just entered the outer court of the king's palace to speak to the king about having Mordekhai hanged on the gallows that he had prepared for him.

5 And the king's young men told him, Haman is there, standing in the court. And the king said, Let him come in.

6 So Haman came in, and the king said to him, What should be done to the man whom the king delights to honor? And Haman said to himself, Whom would the king delight to honor more than me?

7 And Haman said to the king, For the man whom the king delights to honor,

8 Let royal robes be brought, which the king has worn, and the horse that the king has ridden, and on whose head a royal crown is set.

9 And let the robes and the horse be handed over to one of the king's most noble officials. Let them dress the man whom the king delights to honor, and let them lead him on the horse through the square of the city, proclaiming before him: Thus shall it be done to the man whom the king delights to honor.

10 Then the king said to Haman, Hurry. Take the robes and the horse, as you have said, and do so to Mordekhai the Yehudi, who sits at the king's gate. Leave out nothing that you have mentioned.

11 So Haman took the robes and the horse, and he dressed Mordekhai and led him through the square of the city, proclaiming before him, Thus shall it be done to the man whom the king delights to honor.

12 Then Mordekhai returned to the king's gate. But Haman hurried to his house, mourning and with his head covered.

13 And Haman told his wife Zeresh and all his friends everything that had happened to him. Then his wise men and his wife Zeresh said to him, If Mordekhai, before whom you have begun to fall, is of the Jewish people, you will not overcome him but will surely fall before him.

14 While they were yet talking with him, the king's eunuchs arrived and hurried to bring Haman to the feast that Esther had prepared.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. Chapter 6 is the covenant narrative hinge on which the entire covenant reversal turns: the single sleepless night of the covenant king, the covenant record book opened at the covenant right page, the covenant question asked at the covenant moment Haman arrives for his covenant mission of destruction. The covenant chain of covenant coincidences is the covenant literary form that the Masoretic uses to convey covenant providential action without naming YHWH.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 1 — on that night the king could not sleep. The Ge'ez tradition reads the king's insomnia as a covenant divine act: YHWH takes away the king's sleep to set in motion the covenant reversal. The Orit tradition makes explicit what the Masoretic leaves covenant implicit: the sleeplessness is covenant providential, not merely biographical.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 6 — Haman said to himself, Whom would the king delight to honor more than me? The covenant irony of the covenant narrative is complete here: Haman, who came to request Mordekhai's covenant execution, is asked to design the covenant honor ceremony for the man he came to destroy. The covenant plan to destroy Mordekhai becomes the covenant occasion of Mordekhai's honor.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — if Mordekhai, before whom you have begun to fall, is of the Yehudi people, you will not overcome him but will surely fall before him. The covenant recognition by Haman's own advisors: the covenant protection of the covenant Yehudi community is something even the covenant enemy's own inner circle acknowledges. The covenant reversal has begun — and Haman's own covenant advisors pronounce the covenant sentence on him before the covenant king's eunuchs arrive.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 7 The King and Haman Came to the Feast — What Is Your Petition Queen Esther? — She Said An Adversary and Enemy — This Wicked Haman — He Fell on the Couch Where Esther Was — They Hanged Haman on the Gallows

1 So the king and Haman went in to feast with Queen Esther.

2 And on the second day, as they were drinking wine after the feast, the king again said to Esther, What is your wish, Queen Esther? It shall be granted you. And what is your request? Even to the half of my kingdom, it shall be fulfilled.

3 Then Queen Esther answered, If I have found favor in your sight, O king, and if it please the king, let my life be granted me for my wish, and my people for my request.

4 For we have been sold, I and my people, to be destroyed, to be killed, and to be annihilated. If we had been sold merely as slaves, men and women, I would have been silent, for our affliction is not to be compared with the loss to the king.

5 Then King Akhashverosh said to Queen Esther, Who is he, and where is he, who has dared to do this?

6 And Esther said, A foe and enemy — this wicked Haman. Then Haman was terrified before the king and the queen.

7 And the king arose in his wrath from the wine-drinking and went into the palace garden, but Haman stayed to beg for his life from Queen Esther, for he saw that harm was determined against him by the king.

8 And the king returned from the palace garden to the place where they were drinking wine, as Haman was falling on the couch where Esther was. And the king said, Will he even assault the queen in my presence, in my own house? As the word left the mouth of the king, they covered Haman's face.

9 Then Harvona, one of the eunuchs in attendance on the king, said, Moreover, the gallows that Haman has prepared for Mordekhai, whose word saved the king, is standing at Haman's house, fifty cubits high. And the king said, Hang him on that.

10 So they hanged Haman on the gallows that he had prepared for Mordekhai. Then the wrath of the king abated.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. Chapter 7 is the covenant climax of the narrative reversal: Esther's covenant disclosure of covenant identity — verse 3-4 — triggers the covenant cascade that brings down Haman. The covenant woman who concealed her covenant people for the entire preceding narrative now publicly claims them at the covenant moment of maximum covenant danger. The covenant disclosure is the covenant act of covenant courage that the covenant three-day fast prepared.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 3-4 — let my life be granted me for my wish, and my people for my request. For we have been sold to be destroyed, to be killed, and to be annihilated. Esther's covenant petition uses the exact covenant language of Haman's covenant decree — Chapter 3:13 — to destroy, to kill, and to annihilate. She has read the covenant decree. She knows its covenant language. She quotes it back to the king.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 9 — the gallows that Haman has prepared for Mordekhai, whose word saved the king, is standing at Haman's house, fifty cubits high. The covenant eunuch Harvona provides the covenant final piece: the covenant instrument of Mordekhai's intended destruction becomes the covenant instrument of Haman's actual destruction. The covenant gallows that Haman built is the covenant measure of Haman's covenant pride — fifty cubits — and becomes the covenant measure of his covenant fall.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — if we had been sold merely as slaves, I would have been silent, for our affliction is not to be compared with the loss to the king. Esther's covenant rhetorical framing: she presents the covenant issue in terms of covenant loss to the king — not merely covenant injustice to the covenant community. The covenant advocate in the covenant hostile court frames the covenant case in the covenant language the covenant audience will hear. Covenant wisdom requires covenant contextual covenant communication.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 8 Esther Set Mordekhai Over the House of Haman — Write as You Please — The New Decree — Wherever the King's Command Came — Many From the Peoples Became Yehudim

1 On that day King Akhashverosh gave to Queen Esther the house of Haman, the enemy of the Yehudim. And Mordekhai came before the king, for Esther had told what he was to her.

2 And the king took off his signet ring, which he had taken from Haman, and gave it to Mordekhai. And Esther set Mordekhai over the house of Haman.

3 Then Esther spoke again to the king. She fell at his feet and wept and pleaded with him to avert the evil plan of Haman the Agagite and the plot that he had devised against the Yehudim.

4 When the king held out the golden scepter to Esther, Esther rose and stood before the king.

5 And she said, If it please the king, and if I have found favor in his sight, and if the thing seems right before the king, and I am pleasing in his eyes, let an order be written to revoke the letters devised by Haman the Agagite, the son of Hammedata, which he wrote to destroy the Yehudim who are in all the provinces of the king.

6 For how can I bear to see the calamity that is coming to my people? Or how can I bear to see the destruction of my kindred?

7 Then King Akhashverosh said to Queen Esther and to Mordekhai the Yehudi, Behold, I have given Esther the house of Haman, and they have hanged him on the gallows, because he intended to lay hands on the Yehudim.

8 But you may write as you please with regard to the Yehudim, in the name of the king, and seal it with the king's ring, for an edict written in the name of the king and sealed with the king's ring cannot be revoked.

9 The king's scribes were summoned at that time, in the third month, which is the month of Sivan, on the twenty-third day. And an edict was written, according to all that Mordekhai commanded concerning the Yehudim, to the satraps and the governors and the officials of the provinces from Hodu to Kush, 127 provinces, to each province in its own script and to each people in its own language, and also to the Yehudim in their script and their language.

10 And he wrote in the name of King Akhashverosh and sealed it with the king's signet ring. Then he sent the letters by mounted couriers riding on swift horses that were used in the king's service, bred from the royal stud,

11 Saying that the king allowed the Yehudim who were in every city to gather and defend their lives, to destroy, to kill, and to annihilate any armed force of any people or province that might attack them, children and women included, and to plunder their goods,

12 On one day throughout all the provinces of King Akhashverosh, on the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar.

13 A copy of what was written was to be issued as a decree in every province, being publicly displayed to all peoples, and the Yehudim were to be ready on that day to take vengeance on their enemies.

14 So the couriers, mounted on their swift horses that were used in the king's service, rode out hurriedly, urged by the king's command. And the decree was issued in Shushan the citadel.

15 Then Mordekhai went out from the presence of the king in royal robes of blue and white, with a great golden crown and a robe of fine linen and purple, and the city of Shushan shouted and rejoiced.

16 The Yehudim had light and gladness and joy and honor.

17 And in every province and in every city, wherever the king's command and his edict reached, there was gladness and joy among the Yehudim, a feast and a holiday. And many from the peoples of the country declared themselves Yehudim, for fear of the Yehudim had fallen on them.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. Chapter 8 is the covenant reversal at the institutional level: the signet ring that sealed the covenant death decree is now used to seal the covenant counter-decree. The same covenant legal mechanism that Haman used for covenant destruction is now deployed by Mordekhai for covenant defense. The covenant system that produced the covenant threat now produces the covenant rescue.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 17 — many from the peoples of the country declared themselves Yehudim. The covenant universalist outcome of the covenant deliverance: the covenant rescue of the covenant community creates covenant converts from the covenant surrounding peoples. The covenant fear that motivated the covenant original threat — verse 3:8, they are different — now produces covenant identification: the covenant difference that was the covenant grounds of persecution becomes the covenant reason for covenant affiliation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 16 — the Yehudim had light and gladness and joy and honor. The covenant four-fold covenant blessing stands as the covenant opposite of the covenant four-fold covenant threat of Chapter 3:13 — destroy, kill, annihilate, plunder. The covenant narrative reverses every covenant element of the covenant decree.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — to destroy, to kill, and to annihilate any armed force that might attack them. The new decree uses the same covenant language as Haman's decree — Chapter 3:13 — but applies it to the covenant enemies of the covenant community. The covenant language of the covenant original decree is repurposed against its covenant originators. The covenant narrative is not merely the survival of the covenant community but the covenant full inversion of the covenant decree that threatened it.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 9 The Thirteenth Day of Adar — The Yehudim Struck Down Their Enemies — The Sons of Haman Were Hanged — Queen Esther Wrote to Establish Purim — To Observe the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Days

1 Now in the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar, on the thirteenth day of the same, when the king's command and edict were about to be carried out, on the very day when the enemies of the Yehudim hoped to gain the mastery over them, the reverse occurred: the Yehudim gained mastery over those who hated them.

2 The Yehudim gathered in their cities throughout all the provinces of King Akhashverosh to lay hands on those who sought their harm. And no one could stand against them, for the fear of them had fallen on all peoples.

3 All the officials of the provinces and the satraps and the governors and the royal agents also helped the Yehudim, for the fear of Mordekhai had fallen on them.

4 For Mordekhai was great in the king's house, and his fame spread throughout all the provinces, for the man Mordekhai grew more and more powerful.

5 The Yehudim struck all their enemies with the sword, killing and destroying them, and did as they pleased to those who hated them.

6 In Shushan the citadel itself the Yehudim killed and destroyed 500 men,

7 And also killed Parshandata, Dalfon, Aspata,

8 Porata, Adalya, Aridata,

9 Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vaizata,

10 The ten sons of Haman the son of Hammedata, the enemy of the Yehudim, but they did not plunder their goods.

11 That very day the number of those killed in Shushan the citadel was reported to the king.

12 And the king said to Queen Esther, In Shushan the citadel the Yehudim have killed and destroyed 500 men and also the ten sons of Haman. What then have they done in the rest of the king's provinces? Now what is your wish? It shall be granted you. And what further is your request? It shall be fulfilled.

13 And Esther said, If it please the king, let the Yehudim who are in Shushan be allowed tomorrow also to do according to this day's edict. And let the ten sons of Haman be hanged on the gallows.

14 So the king commanded this to be done. A decree was issued in Shushan, and the ten sons of Haman were hanged.

15 The Yehudim who were in Shushan gathered also on the fourteenth day of the month of Adar and they killed 300 men in Shushan, but they did not plunder their goods.

16 Now the rest of the Yehudim who were in the king's provinces also gathered to defend their lives, and got relief from their enemies and killed 75,000 of those who hated them, but they laid no hand on the plunder.

17 This was on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar, and on the fourteenth day they rested and made that a day of feasting and gladness.

18 But the Yehudim who were in Shushan gathered on the thirteenth day and on the fourteenth, and rested on the fifteenth day, making that a day of feasting and gladness.

19 Therefore the Yehudim of the villages, who live in the rural towns, hold the fourteenth day of the month of Adar as a day for gladness and feasting, as a holiday, and as a day on which they send gifts of food to one another.

20 And Mordekhai recorded these things and sent letters to all the Yehudim who were in all the provinces of King Akhashverosh, both near and far,

21 Obligating them to keep the fourteenth day of the month of Adar and also the fifteenth day of the same, year by year,

22 As the days on which the Yehudim got relief from their enemies, and as the month that had been turned for them from sorrow into gladness and from mourning into a holiday. They were to make them days of feasting and gladness, days for sending gifts of food to one another and gifts to the poor.

23 So the Yehudim accepted what they had started to do, and what Mordekhai had written to them.

24 For Haman the Agagite, the son of Hammedata, the enemy of all the Yehudim, had plotted against the Yehudim to destroy them, and had cast Pur — that is, cast the lot — to crush and to destroy them.

25 But when it came before the king, he gave orders in writing that his evil plan that he had devised against the Yehudim should return on his own head, and that he and his sons should be hanged on the gallows.

26 Therefore they called these days Purim, after the term Pur. Therefore, because of all that was written in this letter, and of what they had faced in this matter, and of what had happened to them,

27 The Yehudim firmly obligated themselves and their offspring and all who joined them, that without fail they would keep these two days according to what was written and at the time appointed every year,

28 That these days should be remembered and kept throughout every generation, in every clan, province, and city, and that these days of Purim should never fall into disuse among the Yehudim, nor should the commemoration of these days cease among their descendants.

29 Then Queen Esther, the daughter of Avihayil, and Mordekhai the Yehudi gave full written authority, confirming this second letter about Purim.

30 Letters were sent to all the Yehudim, to the 127 provinces of the kingdom of Akhashverosh, in words of peace and truth,

31 That these days of Purim should be observed at their appointed seasons, as Mordekhai the Yehudi and Queen Esther obligated them, and as they had obligated themselves and their offspring, with regard to their fasts and their lamenting.

32 The command of Esther confirmed these practices of Purim, and it was recorded in writing.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. Chapter 9 establishes the covenant feast of Purim — named after the Pur — the lot that Haman cast — verse 24-26. The covenant feast is the covenant institutionalization of the covenant reversal: the lot cast for the covenant community's destruction becomes the covenant name of the covenant feast celebrating the covenant community's covenant deliverance. The covenant enemy's covenant instrument of covenant decision becomes the covenant community's covenant celebration name.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 22 — the month that had been turned for them from sorrow into gladness and from mourning into a holiday. The covenant transformation of the covenant calendar: Adar, the month chosen by Haman's lot for covenant destruction, becomes the covenant month of covenant celebration. The covenant time consecrated for covenant annihilation is covenantally reconsecrated for covenant joy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 10, 15, 16 — but they did not plunder their goods. The covenant restraint of the covenant community in covenant victory is noted three times: the covenant right to plunder the covenant enemy was granted in the covenant decree of Chapter 8:11 — but the covenant community does not exercise it. The covenant victory is covenant complete without covenant vengeance beyond covenant defense.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 27-28 — they firmly obligated themselves and their offspring and all who joined them, without fail to keep these two days. The covenant obligation extends to all who joined them — the covenant converts of verse 17. The feast of Purim is the covenant feast that reaches beyond the covenant ethnic covenant community to include all who have covenantally joined the covenant people. The covenant feast of the covenant survival of the covenant people becomes the covenant feast of the covenant inclusive covenant community.

ESTHER — CHAPTER 10 King Akhashverosh Imposed Tribute — The Full Account of the High Honor of Mordekhai — Mordekhai the Yehudi Was Second to King Akhashverosh — He Sought the Welfare of His People

1 King Akhashverosh imposed tribute on the land and on the coastlands of the sea.

2 And all the acts of his power and might, and the full account of the high honor of Mordekhai, to which the king advanced him, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the kings of Medes and Persia?

3 For Mordekhai the Yehudi was second in rank to King Akhashverosh, and he was great among the Yehudim and popular with the multitude of his brothers, for he sought the welfare of his people and spoke peace to all his kindred.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Orit Ge'ez is the primary authority. The Ge'ez tradition appends additional material to Chapter 10 — including the interpretation of Mordekhai's dream from the opening of the Ge'ez version — making the covenant theological framework of the book explicit: the two dragons of the dream are Haman and Mordekhai, the nation that cried to YHWH is Yisra'EL, and the covenant dream interpretation confirms that YHWH performed these signs and wonders. The Ge'ez covenant conclusion makes explicit what the Masoretic covenant narrative leaves covenant implicit throughout.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full with the additional dream interpretation. Verse 3 — he sought the welfare of his people and spoke peace to all his kindred. The covenant definition of the covenant diaspora leader's covenant obligation: the covenant official who has risen to covenant power in the covenant foreign court uses that covenant power for the covenant welfare — shalom — of the covenant community. Covenant position in the covenant foreign court is the covenant instrument of covenant community protection.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 10 in three verses — the briefest covenant conclusion in the covenant canon. The covenant book that began with the vast power of the Persian king concludes with the covenant power of the covenant Yehudi: Mordekhai is second only to the covenant king and is beloved by the covenant community because he sought their covenant welfare and spoke covenant peace. The covenant narrative ends with covenant welfare and covenant peace as the covenant fruits of covenant courage.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The book of Esther is the covenant narrative of the covenant diaspora community maintaining covenant identity and covenant survival within covenant hostile covenant structures through covenant wisdom, covenant courage, covenant loyalty, and covenant providential timing. The absence of YHWH's name in the Masoretic — supplemented by the explicit prayer traditions of the Orit Ge'ez — is the covenant literary form that mirrors the covenant hiddenness of YHWH's covenant action in the covenant diaspora: present in every covenant event, named in the covenant prayers, unnamed in the covenant public record.

THE BOOK OF ESTHER — COMPLETE

Volume Twenty-Two — The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

HE SOUGHT THE WELFARE OF HIS PEOPLE AND SPOKE PEACE TO ALL HIS KINDRED

Esther 10:3
